Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the semester. I'd have periods where I barely touched my coursework, then I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours straight, usually when panic set in about an upcoming exam. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts, but I'd often do this at random times rather than on a schedule. The 21 hours I put in weekly were rarely spread evenly—more like concentrated cramming sessions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied wildly depending on deadlines and honestly, my mental state at the time. Some weeks I'd barely study at all, especially when my anxiety was really bad and I couldn't focus. Then as deadlines approached, I'd shift into crisis mode and study intensively. It wasn't healthy, but somehow it worked for my exam scores even if my attendance and assignment completion suffered.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either pure panic about a deadline I'd forgotten or getting a notification about something due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened a lot given my poor attendance. Occasionally I'd go in because I was having a better day mentally and wanted to catch up, but those moments were less frequent than I'd like to admit.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a true routine for me. There were brief periods, maybe a week or two, where I'd check in more regularly after my CBT sessions when I was feeling more in control. But then something would throw me off—stress from other responsibilities or my anxiety spiking—and the routine would fall apart. It was more like occasional habits that never quite stuck.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and articles and annotate them heavily on my own time. The discussion posts were also surprisingly helpful when I actually engaged with them—reading what my peers thought helped me understand concepts from different angles. Videos were okay but I preferred having text I could highlight and revisit.
Q6: I often delayed watching videos because they felt time-consuming and I couldn't skim them like I could with readings. Some of the optional supplementary materials I ignored completely because I was already overwhelmed trying to keep up with the essentials. I also didn't engage with certain interactive activities because when my anxiety was high, anything that felt like extra work just got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam content or assignments. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, I'd invest time in it. Also, activities that I could do at my own pace, like readings I could annotate, felt more valuable than things that required real-time engagement when I might not be in the right headspace.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones created pressure that sometimes helped me push through my anxiety, but also sometimes paralyzed me. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later rarely came. There was less guilt attached to skipping optional things, which made them easier to ignore even when they might have been helpful.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and notes, print them out sometimes, and work through them with a pen in hand away from my computer. I also used textbooks from the library and created my own handwritten summaries. My best studying often happened when I was disconnected from everything, just me and my notes, because the internet could be distracting when I was already anxious.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials without logging into the LMS at all. I'd also study with classmates in person—we'd quiz each other or discuss concepts—and none of that showed up anywhere. Some of my most productive study sessions happened at a café with my notebook, completely offline.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd log in, download everything I needed in one go, and then work through it all offline over the following days. I preferred studying without the distraction of being online, plus it helped my anxiety to not constantly see reminders of what I hadn't done yet. One download might fuel several days of actual study time.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really hard, usually when my anxiety peaked or when I'd fallen so far behind that catching up felt impossible. Around midterms, I remember feeling completely overwhelmed—my attendance had dropped, assignments were piling up, and I just wanted to hide from it all. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that, but those dark periods were real.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. When I was fatigued or stressed, the LMS felt like this overwhelming portal of obligations I couldn't meet. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them. My extracurricular commitments sometimes competed for my energy, and when I was already running on empty emotionally, the coursework often lost that battle.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through materials without really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere or I was too anxious to focus. I'd watch videos while my thoughts raced about other things. I'd have tabs open with course content but spend the time staring at the screen without processing. Those sessions probably looked like engagement but they weren't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—there was this fear of doing it wrong that paralyzed me. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a frustrating cycle where the avoidance actually made my anxiety worse, which made starting even harder.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd operate in crisis mode, logging in frantically as due dates approached, trying to piece together what I'd missed. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have completed anything. They created the pressure I needed to push through my avoidance, though the quality of work done in panic wasn't always my best.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when it was positive—seeing that my exam scores were good despite everything gave me hope that I wasn't a complete failure. But negative feedback or criticism on assignments sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. After my CBT sessions, I got better at handling feedback without catastrophizing, which helped my mood improve.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules helped me understand what I needed to cover, but the pacing assumed consistent engagement that I couldn't maintain. When I fell behind, the structure made it clear just how much I'd missed, which was overwhelming. I wished there was more flexibility built in for students like me who struggle with consistency.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could work offline. But the emphasis on regular attendance and consistent assignment submission was really hard for me. Discussion requirements felt stressful when I hadn't been keeping up with the content. More self-paced options or understanding around irregular engagement patterns would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking through things, trying to engage but being too anxious or overwhelmed to actually complete anything. Sometimes you're there, you're trying, but the actual output just isn't happening. It could also be someone who's researching and preparing but hasn't reached the submission stage yet.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I got a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials once, study them extensively on my own, and then perform well on exams. The logs would show minimal engagement, but the knowledge was there. It just wasn't acquired in ways the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion of specific tasks, which gives some picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone was truly focused or just going through the motions. They also miss offline study, emotional struggles that affect participation, and the difference between someone who learns quickly versus someone who needs more time but is equally engaged.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, when concepts connect in my mind, when I want to learn more. It doesn't necessarily mean logging in constantly or attending every class. Some of my most engaged moments happened alone with my notes, wrestling with ideas until they made sense.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, the hours spent building courage to start an assignment, or the relief after a CBT session that finally let me focus. It wouldn't show my genuine interest in the subject despite my erratic participation, or how much I learned from offline study. It definitely wouldn't capture that I was struggling but still trying, or that my Distinction came from real understanding, not just system compliance.
